title: Jam roly-poly
subtitle: Suet pastry rolled with jam and baked until golden.
excerpt: A soft suet pastry spread with raspberry jam, rolled into a log and baked in a loose foil parcel until steamed through and lightly golden. One of the most satisfying British puddings, served with custard. Serves four to six.
sourceNotes: Jam roly-poly. The suet roll pudding appears in British cookbooks throughout the 19th century under various names including 'roly-poly pudding' and 'jam pudding roll'. Mrs Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861, public domain) documents several suet roll puddings. The oven method used here produces the same result as steaming with considerably less effort.

content.text: Suet gives roly-poly pastry a unique tenderness: it does not need careful handling or chilling, and the result is denser and more yielding than shortcrust. Lard is the closest widely-available substitute for traditional beef or vegetable suet. The foil parcel creates a gentle steaming environment in the oven, keeping the pudding moist throughout.

Heat the oven to 180°C. Mix {{self-raising-flour}} of self-raising flour, {{lard}} of grated or crumbled lard and {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt together. Add {{whole-milk}} of milk a little at a time until you have a soft, slightly sticky dough. Roll out on a floured surface to a rectangle roughly 25 x 30 cm. Spread {{jam}} of jam over the surface, leaving a 2 cm border at one short edge.

Roll the dough from the jamless short edge into a tight log, pressing the seam gently to seal. Wrap loosely in buttered baking foil, folding the ends to seal but leaving some space for expansion. Place on a baking tray and bake at 180°C for 35 to 40 minutes until cooked through. Rest for 5 minutes before unwrapping. Slice thickly and serve with custard.

items.cause: Seam not sealed, or too much jam.
items.fix: Press the seam firmly before wrapping. 150 g of jam is the maximum for this quantity of pastry.
Add milk gradually — suet dough needs less than you think. Mix only until combined.

items.cause: Foil wrapped too tightly, creating steam rather than dry heat.
items.fix: Open the foil for the last 5 minutes to give a little colour. The inside will stay moist.

content.text: Jam roly-poly is a schoolroom pudding that has survived into home kitchens because it is straightforwardly good. The dough is tolerant, the result is reliably comforting, and the whole thing takes half an hour from start to oven. Raspberry jam is traditional; apricot, plum or gooseberry work equally well. Always serve with proper poured custard.
